No, you should only refresh the JWT access token when a user action or a pending request triggers it, rather than background-refreshing immediately upon expiration.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

How Refresh Token WorksIn client-side development, there are three primary approaches to trigger a refresh token flow to acquire a new access token. These approaches range from reactive strategies (handling the failure after it happens) to proactive strategies (preventing the failure entirely).
1. Reactive Approach: Intercepting a 401 Unauthorized Response
The application makes API calls normally. If a call fails because the access token expired, a middleware interceptor catches the failure and silently refreshes the token.
How it works:

• The client triggers a standard data API request.

• The server rejects it with a 401 Unauthorized status.

• A network interceptor (like an HTTP interceptor or axios interceptor) catches the 401.

• The interceptor pauses the failing request, triggers the refresh token API, updates the storage with the new access token, and retries the original request.
Pros: Robust, resilient to clock desynchronization, and acts as a safety net.
Cons: Introduces network latency because every expiration causes at least one failed request and a subsequent retry.
2. Proactive Approach: Pre-emptive Expiration Check (Internal Validation)
The client checks the lifespan of the access token locally before making any API request. If the token is about to expire, it triggers the refresh workflow first.
How it works:

• The client decodes the access token payload to extract its expiration timestamp (exp).

• Before every API call, the client compares exp against the current device system time.

• If the token has expired — or is within a “buffer zone” (e.g., less than 30 seconds left) — the client automatically hits the refresh endpoint before sending the actual API request.
Pros: Seamless user experience with zero failed requests due to expiration.
Cons: Highly vulnerable to client-side clock drift (if the user’s phone or computer clock is inaccurate, the calculation breaks).
3. Automated Approach: Background Timer Execution
The client schedules a persistent background timer to trigger the refresh token request right before the token is scheduled to die.
How it works:

• Upon a successful login or previous refresh, the client calculates the token’s lifetime remaining (expires_in).

• The client initializes an asynchronous background worker or timer (e.g., setTimeout or a Web Worker).

• The timer executes automatically 1 to 2 minutes before the token expires, updating the global state or local storage with a fresh token pair. [1, 2, 3]
Pros: Completely independent of API traffic; tokens remain fresh even if the user is just reading a page without making API requests.
Cons: Can waste server resources and API bandwidth if the user leaves the tab open but goes idle (the app will keep refreshing tokens forever unless an idle-detection script stops it).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Architectural ComparisonImportant Concurrency Note: For all three approaches, if your application fires multiple asynchronous API requests at the exact same moment when a token expires, it can trigger multiple concurrent refresh token requests. To avoid this, always implement a request queue lock so that only one refresh process executes at a time while the other requests wait.
Why Automated Approach: Background Timer Execution is a Bad Idea ?

• Wasted Server Resources: If a user leaves your app open in a background tab and walks away, an automated timer will continuously ping your server every 15 minutes to rotate tokens for an idle session.

• Keeps Stale Sessions Alive: It defeats the purpose of absolute session timeouts. The app will stay logged in forever unless you build complex idle-detection logic to halt the timer.

• Security Risk: If an attacker steals a device or a session is left unattended, a continuous background refresh mechanism ensures the session stays active indefinitely.
When Automated Approach: Background Timer Execution Is Acceptable ?
The only time you should proactively refresh a token without a manual request is if you have an active web socket connection or a live-updating dashboard that pulls data continuously. In that specific scenario, the app is technically making background requests anyway.
